message.content: What is the standard surface gravity of Mars in meters per second squared?

arguments.query: standard surface gravity of Mars m/s² meters per second squared

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Earth_vs_Mars_gravity_at_elevation.webp[Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth) vs [Mars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars) vs [Moon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitation_of_the_Moon) [gravity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity) at [elevation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elevation)

The gravity of Mars is a natural phenomenon, due to the [law of gravity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_gravity), or gravitation, by which all things with mass around the planet [Mars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars) are brought towards it. It is weaker than [Earth's gravity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth) due to the planet's smaller mass. The average [gravitational acceleration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_acceleration) on Mars is 3.721 m/s2 (about 38% of the [gravity of Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth)), with a global variation range of ~0.059 m/s2.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

In general, topography-controlled [isostasy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isostasy) drives the short wavelength free-air [gravity anomalies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity_anomaly).[[2]](#cite_note-:23-2) At the same time, [convective flow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convective_flow) and finite strength of the mantle lead to long-wavelength planetary-scale [free-air gravity anomalies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free-air_gravity_anomaly) over the entire planet.[[3]](#cite_note-:21-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:20-4) Variation in crustal thickness, magmatic and volcanic activities, impact-induced [Moho](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mohorovičić_discontinuity)-uplift, seasonal variation of polar ice caps, atmospheric mass variation and variation of [porosity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porosity) of the crust could also correlate to the lateral variations.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:18-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:14-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:19-9)

Over the years models consisting of an increasing but limited number of [spherical harmonics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spherical_harmonics) have been produced. Maps produced have included [free-air gravity anomaly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free-air_gravity_anomaly), [Bouguer gravity anomaly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bouguer_gravity_anomaly), and crustal thickness. In some areas of Mars there is a correlation between gravity anomalies and topography. Given the known topography, higher resolution gravity field can be inferred. Tidal deformation of Mars by the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun) or [Phobos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phobos_(moon)) can be measured by its gravity. This reveals how stiff the interior is, and shows that the core is partially liquid.
The study of surface gravity of Mars can therefore yield information about different features and provide beneficial information for future [Mars landings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars_landing).

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Rotating_spherical_harmonics.gifRotating spherical harmonic, with for the vertical, and for the horizontal. For the Martian C20 and C30, they vary with time because of the seasonal variation of mass of the polar ice caps through the annual sublimation-condensation cycle of carbon dioxide.

To understand the gravity of Mars, its [gravitational field strength](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_field_strength) g and gravitational potential U are often measured. Simply, if Mars is assumed to be a static perfectly spherical body of radius RM, provided that there is only one satellite revolving around Mars in a circular orbit and such gravitation interaction is the only force acting in the system, the equation would be

where G is the [universal constant of gravitation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_constant) (commonly taken as G = 6.674 × 10−11 m3 kg−1 s−2),[[10]](#cite_note-10) M is the mass of Mars (most updated value: 6.41693 × 1023 kg),[[11]](#cite_note-11) m is the mass of the satellite, r is the distance between Mars and the satellite, and is the [angular velocity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_velocity) of the satellite, which is also equivalent to (T is the orbiting period of the satellite).

Therefore, , where RM is the radius of Mars. With proper measurement, r, T, and RM are obtainable parameters from Earth.

However, as Mars is a generic, non-spherical planetary body and influenced by complex geological processes, more accurately, the [gravitational potential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_potential) is described with [spherical harmonic functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spherical_harmonics), following convention in geodesy; see [Geopotential model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geopotential_model).

[[12]](#cite_note-:7-12)

where are spherical coordinates of the test point.[[12]](#cite_note-:7-12) is longitude and is latitude. and are dimensionless harmonic coefficients of degree and order .[[12]](#cite_note-:7-12) is the Legendre polynomial of degree with
and is the associated Legendre polynomial with . These are used to describe solutions of [Laplace's equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laplace's_equation).[[12]](#cite_note-:7-12) is the mean radius of the planet.[[12]](#cite_note-:7-12)
The coefficient is sometimes written as .

- The lower the degree and order , the longer wavelength of anomaly it represents. In turn, long-wavelength gravity anomaly is influenced by global geophysical structures.

- The higher the degree and order , the shorter wavelength of anomaly it represents. For degree over 50, it has been shown that those variations have high correlation with the topography.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) Geophysical interpretation of surface features could further help deriving a more complete picture of the Martian gravity field, though misleading results could be produced.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13)

The oldest technique in determining the gravity of Mars is through Earth-based observation. Later with the arrival of uncrewed spacecraft, subsequent gravity models were developed from radio tracking data.
Scientists measured the perturbation of different spacecraft through Doppler and range tracking methods to develop different gravity models ever since the first arrival of the uncrewed space probe [Mariner 9](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariner_9) in 1971. (Credit: NASA's Scientific Visualization Studio)

### Earth-based observation
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravity_of_Mars&action=edit&section=2)]

Before the arrival of the [Mariner 9](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariner_9) and [Viking orbiter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viking_orbiter) spacecraft at Mars, only an estimate of the Mars gravitational constant GM, i.e. the [universal constant of gravitation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_constant) times the mass of Mars, was available for deducing the properties of the Martian gravity field.[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14) GM could be obtained through observations of the motions of the natural satellites of Mars ([Phobos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phobos_(moon)) and [Deimos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deimos_(moon))) and spacecraft flybys of Mars ([Mariner 4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariner_4) and [Mariner 6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariner_6)).[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14)

Long term Earth-based observations of the motions of Phobos and Deimos provide physical parameters including [semi-major axis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semi-major_axis), [eccentricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity), inclination angle to the [Laplacian plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laplacian_plane) etc.,[[15]](#cite_note-:24-15) which allow calculation of the ratio of solar mass to the mass of Mars, [moment of inertia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moment_of_inertia) and coefficient of the gravitational potential of Mars, and give initial estimates of the gravity field of Mars.[[15]](#cite_note-:24-15)

### Inferred from radio tracking data
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravity_of_Mars&action=edit&section=3)]

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Three_way_doppler.jpgThree-way Doppler, with signal transmitter and receiver separated
Precise tracking of spacecraft is of prime importance for accurate gravity modeling, as gravity models are developed from observing tiny perturbation of spacecraft, i.e. small variation in velocity and altitude. The tracking is done basically by the antennae of the [Deep Space Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_Space_Network) (DSN), with one-way, two-way and three-way Doppler and range tracking applied.[[16]](#cite_note-:25-16) One-way tracking means the data is transmitted in one way to the DSN from the spacecraft, while two-way and three-way involve transmitting signals from Earth to the spacecraft (uplink), and thereafter transponded coherently back to the Earth (downlink).[[16]](#cite_note-:25-16) The difference between two-way and three-way tracking is, the former one has the same signal transmitter and receiver on Earth, while the latter one has the transmitter and receiver at different locations on Earth.[[16]](#cite_note-:25-16) The use of these three types of tracking data enhances the coverage and quality of the data, as one could fill in the data gap of another.[[16]](#cite_note-:25-16)

Doppler tracking is a common technique in tracking the spacecraft, utilizing radial velocity method, which involves detection of Doppler shifts.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) As the spacecraft moves away from us along line of sight, there would be redshift of signal, while for the reverse, there would be blueshift of signal. Such technique has also been applied for observation of the motion of exoplanets.[[17]](#cite_note-17) While for the range tracking, it is done through measurement of round trip propagation time of the signal.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) Combination of [Doppler shift](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doppler_shift) and range observation promotes higher tracking accuracy of the spacecraft.

The tracking data would then be converted to develop global gravity models using the spherical harmonic equation displayed above. However, further elimination of the effects due to affect of [solid tide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_tide), various relativistic effects due to the Sun, Jupiter and Saturn, [non-conservative forces](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-conservative_force) (e.g. [angular momentum desaturations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_momentum_desaturation?action=edit&redlink=1) (AMD), [atmospheric drag](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atmospheric_drag) and [solar radiation pressure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_radiation_pressure)) have to be done,[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) otherwise, considerable errors result.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w.svg
This section is missing information about alternative nomenclature such as GMM-3 as mgm1025, used in some NASA papers and in its planetary data system. Please expand the section to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Gravity_of_Mars). (January 2022)

The latest gravity model for Mars is the Goddard Mars Model 3 (GMM-3), produced in 2016, with spherical harmonics solution up to degree and order 120.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) This model is developed from 16 years of radio tracking data from Mars Global Surveyor (MGS), Mars Odyssey and Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter (MRO), as well as the MOLA topography model and provides a global resolution of 115 km.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) A separate free-air gravity anomaly map, Bouguer gravity anomaly map and a map of crustal thickness were produced along with this model.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) Compared with MRO110C and other previous models, major improvement of the estimation of the gravity field comes from more careful modeling of the non-conservative forces applied to the spacecraft.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13)

The techniques in tracking the spacecraft and geophysical interpretation of surface features can affect the resolution of the strength of gravity field. The better technique favors spherical harmonic solutions to higher degrees and orders. Independent analysis on [Mariner 9](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariner_9) and [Viking Orbiter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viking_program) tracking data yielded a degree and order of 6 spherical harmonic solution.,[[18]](#cite_note-:8-18) Further combination of the two data sets, along with correlation of anomalies with volcanic features (positive anomaly) and deep-printed depression (negative anomaly) assisted by image data allows a degree and order of 18 spherical harmonic solution produced.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-19) Further use of spatial a priori constraint method, which had taken the topography into account in solving the Kaula power law constraint, had favored model of up to degree 50 spherical harmonic solution in global resolution ([Goddard Mars Model-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goddard_Mars_Model-1?action=edit&redlink=1), or GMM-1)[[20]](#cite_note-:10-20) then the subsequent models with higher completeness and degree and order up to 120 for the latest GMM-3.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:GMM-3_Free_air_gravity_anomaly.jpgMars free-air gravity map produced along with the GMM-3 gravity solution[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) (Red: gravity high; Blue: gravity low) (Credit: NASA's Scientific Visualization Studio)

Therefore, gravity models nowadays are not directly produced through transfer of the measured gravity data to any spatial information system because there is difficulty in producing model with sufficiently high resolution. [Topography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topography) data obtained from the [MOLA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars_Orbiter_Laser_Altimeter) instrument aboard the Mars Global Surveyor thus becomes a useful tool in producing a more detailed short-scale gravity model, utilizing the gravity-topography correlation in short-wavelength.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) However, not all regions on Mars show such correlation, notably the northern lowland and the poles.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) Misleading results could be easily produced, which could lead to wrong geophysics interpretation.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13)

The later modifications of gravity model include taking other non-conservative forces acting on spacecraft into account, including [atmospheric drag](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atmospheric_drag), [solar radiation pressure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_radiation_pressure), Mars reflected [solar radiation pressure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_radiation_pressure), Mars [thermal emission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_radiation), and spacecraft thrusting which despins or desaturates the [angular moment wheels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Momentum_wheel).[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14) In addition, Martian [precession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precession) and third body attraction due to the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun), [Moon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moon) and planets, which could affect the spacecraft orbit, as well as [relavistic effects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relavistic_effect?action=edit&redlink=1) on the measurements should also be corrected.[[7]](#cite_note-:14-7) These factors could lead to offset of the true gravity field. Accurate modeling is thus required to eliminate the offset. Such work is still ongoing.

## Static gravity field
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravity_of_Mars&action=edit&section=5)]

Many researchers have outlined the correlation between short-wavelength (locally varying) free-air gravity anomalies and topography. For regions with higher correlation, free-air gravity anomalies could be expanded to higher degree strength through geophysical interpretation of surface features,[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) so that the gravity map could offer higher resolution. It has been found that the southern highland has high gravity/topography correlation but not for the northern lowland.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13) Therefore, the resolution of free-air gravity anomaly model typically has higher resolution for the southern hemisphere, as high as over 100 km.[[13]](#cite_note-:4-13)

Free-air gravity anomalies a

Based on the Wikipedia article, the standard surface gravity of Mars is **3.721 m/s²** (meters per second squared).

This is approximately 38% of Earth's gravity (which is 9.807 m/s²). There's also a global variation range of about ±0.059 m/s² across the planet due to factors like topography, crustal thickness, and polar ice cap variations.
